---
title: "Lab Template"
author: 
  - name: "Sample Student"
    affiliation: "DATA 202, Fall 2024, Howard University"
date: "2024-09-07"
always_allow_html: true
output: 
  html_document:
    code-copy: true
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 2
    toc_float:
      collapsed: false
      smooth_scroll: true
    number_sections: true
    self_contained: yes
    mode: selfcontained
  pdf_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 2
    number_sections: true
geometry: margin=1.0in
editor_options:
  markdown:
    wrap: sentence
---

# Preparing a lab report

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
# install.packages("reticulate",repos = "http://cran.us.r-project.org")
install.packages("educationdata",repos = "http://cran.us.r-project.org")
library(reticulate)
library(educationdata)
```

## Using R Markdown

In RStudio, open an R Markdown document by selecting File \> New File \> R Markdown.
When the dialog box opens, you can add a preliminary name to the file (but this is not yet required until you save the file), so you can simply click 'OK'.
At the top of the R Markdown document, enter the following code (include the three dashes at the top and bottom):

```         
---
title: "Lab No. #"
author: 
  - name: "Your Name"
    affiliation: "DATA 202, Fall 2024, Howard University"
date: "2024-09-07"
always_allow_html: true
output: 
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 2
    number_sections: true
    self_contained: yes
    mode: selfcontained
  pdf_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 2
    number_sections: true
geometry: margin=1.0in
---
```

This is an R Markdown document.
Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents.
For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document.
You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
summary(cars)
```

## Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r pressure, echo=FALSE}
plot(pressure)
```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.

```{python}
x = [1, 2, 3]
```

```{r}
py$x
```

```{r}
py$y <- head(cars)
```

```{python}
print(y)
```

# Using different code {.tabset}

## R

(tab content)

## Python

(tab content)
